articles.description: On 1 July, an Airbus A330 lifts off from Harare for London under the

articles.title: Zimbabwe's tobacco anchor in Britain holds at £129m
articles.description: Of every £100 of goods Zimbabwe sells to Britain, £96 is tobacco or beverages. While the wider UK–Zimbabwe trade relationship softened in the year to December 2025, Zimbabwean tobacco held its grip, and the IMF projects Zimbabwean GDP growth of 7.5% in 2025 and 5.0% in 2026. The product that built Zimbabwe's name as an exporter is still doing the work.

articles.title: The Apple of haircare gets its German cheque
articles.description: Ruka, the London-based textured-hair company founded by Zimbabwean entrepreneur Varaidzo Tendai Moyo and her co-founder Ugorji Agbai, has closed a $4.5m round co-led by Henkel Ventures and Freedom Trail Capital. The deal, which takes lifetime funding to roughly $10m and brings one of the world's largest consumer-goods groups onto the cap table, is the most meaningful endorsement yet of a Zimbabwean diaspora-founded brand.

more.title: Exclusive: Air Zimbabwe Rebuilds Its Booking Engine Before London, in Silence
more.description: Days before its London return, Air Zimbabwe has switched on a new

more.title: Pardon Mapondera: gone but not forgotten at Venice
more.description: The fifth and final piece in our series on Zimbabwe's pavilion at the 61st Venice Biennale. Pardon Mapondera, the artist whose practice the room finally arranges itself around, treats discarded plastic and glass jars not as ecological signal but as ritual matter, and produces, in the process, the most quietly devotional installation in the Biennale this year.

more.title: Gideon Gomo: from stone to bronze, the ancestral algorithm at Venice
more.description: The fourth in a five-part series on Zimbabwe's pavilion at the 61st Venice Biennale. Gideon Gomo's first sustained body of bronze work, paired with discarded vehicle number plates and Zimbabwean springstone, is the move of a sculptor at full maturity rethinking the material vocabulary of his country's most internationally legible art form.

more.title: Shumba Maasai in Venice
more.description: At the 61st Biennale, the Zimbabwean artist's day-long procession through the city's campi, performed under the borrowed authority of a Murehwa chief, restages the social-sculpture lineage of Joseph Beuys for a contemporary African discursive practice.

more.title: Franklyn Dzingai: family albums and the neural commons at Venice
more.description: The third in a five-part series on Zimbabwe's pavilion at the 61st Venice Biennale. Franklyn Dzingai's reductive cardboard prints, built from the family album, the magazine clipping, the back-room studio photographs of mid-century Zimbabwe, turn out, on close looking, to be the most precisely engineered work in the room.

more.title: Felix Shumba: charcoal and the practice of disappearance at Venice
more.description: The second in a five-part series on Zimbabwe's pavilion at the 61st Venice Biennale. Felix Shumba's six-metre charcoal wall and his Telephone Reader series anchor a body of work that argues drawing is not the act of capturing the seen, but the act of staying present to what is on its way out of sight.

more.title: UK services share in Zimbabwe rises to 8.2%
more.description: The headline UK services trade with Zimbabwe softened year on year. The structural number underneath it tells the better story: Britain's share of all services Zimbabwe imports rose 2.4 percentage points to 8.2%, the highest measured level in years. As Zimbabwean buyers re-rate their global supplier mix, the UK is taking a bigger slice of the country's services demand.

more.title: Zimbabwe's demand lifts British exports by 14.6%
more.description: The Zimbabwean economy is in its strongest stretch in years: 7.5% GDP growth in 2025, a projected 5% in 2026, and that growth is pulling demand for cars, commercial vehicles, medicines and industrial machinery in with it. UK goods exports to Zimbabwe rose 14.6% on the year, the strongest line in an otherwise soft bilateral picture for Britain.

more.title: Four miners die after shaft collapse
more.description: A MINING disaster in Chinhoyi, Mashonaland West Province, has claimed the lives of four miners after a shaft at Rowdy Mine collapsed, trapping workers underground. Rescue teams recovered the final two bodies on Saturday morning after an overnight operation, bringing the death toll to four. The accident happened on Thursday at the mine, commonly known [...] The post Four miners die after shaft collapse appeared first on NewZimbabwe.com .

more.title: Preach hard work & production, President tells Church
more.description: Samuel Kadungure Mutare Bureau PRESIDENT MNANGAGWA yesterday challenged the Church to encourage a culture of hard, honest work and productivity as the country accelerates its economic transformation and development agenda. Addressing thousands of local and international pilgrims gathered for the Johane Marange Apostolic Church’s Big Sabbath Service at the Mafararikwa Shrine in Marange, Manicaland province, yesterday, the President said the Church had a critical role to play in promoting productivity and supporting national development efforts. The service, which preceded the church’s annual Passover (Pasika) gathering, attracted thousands of worshippers from across Zimbabwe and beyond. President Mnangagwa said the country’s economic aspirations could only be realised if citizens across all sectors, including religious communities, actively participated in productive activities that create wealth, improve livelihoods and strengthen food security. “Our nation is marching on to Vision 2030. I challenge the Church to make loud its voice and encourage hard, honest work, production and productivity in all sectors, as we develop our country,” said President Mnangagwa. “Kereke ngaiparidze kukosha kwekushanda nesimba. Ngaiparidze rudo, kubatana nekuda nyika yedu. Mwari akataura kuti tichadya cheziya. (The Church should preach the importance of hard work. It must preach love, unity and patriotism. God says by the sweat of your brow you will eat your food.) Our national culture, values and identity as well as the correct moral upbringing of our children are important. “The Church has a part to play. Tinovimba nekereke kuti ibatsire kuti tirambe tichichengetedza tsika dzedu nehunhu hwakanaka senyika. Ndimi chiyedza chenyika; rambai muchibwinya (I hope the Church will help us preserve our cultural values and norms as a country. You are the light of the world; continue shining).” President Mnangagwa also said the Church should continue providing spiritual guidance while encouraging its members to contribute meaningfully to national development through enterprise, innovation and increased production. He commended the Johane Marange Apostolic Church for investing in key sectors such as education, entrepreneurship, skills development, sports, recreation and mining, saying the initiatives were helping to improve livelihoods and stimulate economic growth. “That is commendable,” he said. He encouraged the church to expand its empowerment projects, describing them as practical examples of how faith-based organisations can contribute to national development. “The many empowerment projects you have undertaken demonstrate how your church contributes towards the growth of our economy. “Tinotenda. Well done.” The Government, he added, would continue implementing reforms designed to remove barriers to production and support the growth of businesses and entrepreneurship. “Hurumende icharamba ichidzikisa mitero yemalicences and levies kuti vanhu vawane kushanda zvakareruka (The Government will continue to review downwards fees and levies to improve the ease of doing business),” he said. “Our ZANU PF party policies will continue to promote access to more economic opportunities, wealth creation, jobs and a better quality of life for our people. “Mhuri yeZimbabwe uyai, ngativakeizve nyika yekwedu, kuti tinyadzise vavengi vedu. (Let’s come together to build our country as Zimbabweans in order to put our enemies to shame). I call upon the Church to mobilise and organise congregants to participate in empowerment programmes being championed by the Government. Vemakereke, musasarire kumashure (The Church must not be left behind). “Let us all be frontrunners in building the Zimbabwe we all want, household by household, village by village, ward by ward and district by district.” The President also called for increased focus on agriculture and food security, urging farmers to prepare for possible climate-related challenges, including an El Niño-induced drought, by increasing the production of traditional grains and adopting climate-resilient farming practices. He reiterated that under the ZANU PF-led Government, no one and no place would be left behind, stressing that food security remained a national priority. President Mnangagwa also applauded Manicaland province for its growing contribution to the national economy, citing achievements in wheat production, dairy farming and manufacturing. He encouraged businesses and communities in the province to embrace technology, expand exports and take advantage of emerging economic opportunities to further drive growth. The President thanked John Marange Apostolic Church high priest and spiritual leader Mutumwa St Nimrod Taguta, church leaders, traditional leaders and ZANU PF officials for promoting peace, unity and prayer for Zimbabwe and the African continent. President Mnangagwa was accompanied by Chief Secretary to the President and Cabinet Dr Martin Rushwaya, Defence Minister Oppah Muchinguri-Kashiri, Minister of State for Manicaland Provincial Affairs and Devolution Advocate Misheck Mugadza, Youth Empowerment, Development and Vocational Training Minister Tino Machakaire, legislators and senior Government officials. The annual Passover gathering, held from July 1 to 17, is one of the largest religious events on the church’s calendar and attracts thousands of pilgrims from Zimbabwe and neighbouring countries.

more.title: STEVE MAKONI STILL IN THE GAME
more.description: Trust Khosa FEW Zimbabwean entertainers have reinvented themselves as successfully and consistently as Stephen “Steve” Makoni. Over the decades, he has worn many hats with remarkable ease, proving that talent, discipline and perseverance can coexist in one extraordinary individual. Whether on stage or behind the scenes, Makoni has always relentlessly pursued excellence. His story is a powerful reminder that success is not confined to a single profession. While many Zimbabweans know him as the celebrated musician behind several timeless hits, his journey extends far beyond music, reflecting a man whose passion for learning and creativity has shaped an inspiring career. Simply known as Steve Makoni or Dhongi, the veteran entertainer is a man of many talents. From comedy and theatre to filmmaking and music, Makoni has excelled in virtually every field he has pursued. Academically gifted, he performed consistently well throughout his studies, eventually becoming a qualified laboratory scientist — a profession he practised at a local pharmacy. His scientific background reflects the versatility that has defined his remarkable life. Fans fondly remember him for classics such as “Handiende”, “Zvachonyana”, “Haungadaro” and “Chaenda Nechimwe Chii?” Beyond his recordings, Makoni possesses an infectious personality that leaves a lasting impression on everyone he meets. Every interaction with him is both exciting and enlightening, as he effortlessly shares the wisdom, humour and life experiences accumulated over a distinguished career. As a performer, he seamlessly blends comedy with music, creating a stage presence that has endeared him to audiences for decades. His ability to connect with both strangers and old friends with warmth and authenticity is a rare gift, distinguishing him from many of his contemporaries. Now 72, Makoni, who is still as fit as a fiddle, has retreated to his family farm in Rusape. “I spend most of my time here in Rusape, at Farm Number 56, a family business that I now run,” he told the Zimpapers Arts and Entertainment Hub. “I thoroughly enjoy everything I am doing here because farming is also my passion. I am into horticulture and poultry production, specialising in road-runner chickens. It is a family enterprise and I am glad to be fulfilling my late father’s wish to ensure continuity and productivity at the farm. “He was an entrepreneur par excellence and I am simply following in his footsteps. My dream is to make him proud.” Raised in a family of 11 children, the artiste said hard work has been his guiding principle since childhood. “I am a hard worker who loves his job to bits. I will continue doing my best to ensure that I achieve my dreams . . .” Reflecting on his academic path, Makoni recalls his formative years. “I completed my Advanced Level in 1975, studying Biology, Literature and Geography through correspondence. I later trained as a laboratory scientist and worked at Pfizer Pharmacy from 1980 to 1991, while also studying at Harare Polytechnic to further hone my skills. During our time, becoming a professional was everyone’s dream and I can safely say I thoroughly enjoyed my career,” he said. Meeting Tuku His musical journey took shape after meeting his late friend, Oliver “Tuku” Mtukudzi. “I started playing the guitar while I was still at school and meeting Tuku completely changed my perception of music,” he said. “We experimented with many things, including theatre and filmmaking. We even formed a theatre company before eventually concentrating on music. “One of our plays was called ‘My Child’, which was all about celebrating life and having fun back in the day. We had high expectations, but in the end, we decided to focus on music.” The multi-talented artiste believes his major breakthrough came in the early 1990s. “My songs ‘Handiende’ and ‘Maidei’ marked my breakthrough. In 1994, ‘Handiende’ was voted one of the year’s best singles — the same year Leonard Zhakata dominated the charts with ‘Maruva Enyika’.” The popularity of “Handiende” earned Makoni a residency at Midlands Hotel in Gweru, where he performed for two years. “Between 1994 and 1996, I was in high demand. My brand became so popular that I eventually relocated to Harare, performing at several hotels, including New Ambassador and Monomotapa. My solo performances endeared me to fans and even today, I still miss those moments.” However, family tragedies in the late 1990s forced him to reconsider his priorities. “In 1998, I lost my father, and within a year, my brother, Phinius, passed away. Another bereavement followed in 2000, leaving me with no option but to relocate to Rusape in 2002 to take over the family business. Since then, I have been living here and I truly enjoy life. I cannot thank God enough for the gift of life that has allowed me to witness so many more years.” At the peak of his career, Makoni also toured Europe, particularly England and Sweden, performing for both Zimbabwean and international audiences. Today, his legacy is measured not just by his recordings or the stages he has graced, but by the inspiration he offers through a life defined by resilience, versatility and excellence. As Zimbabwe celebrates one of its finest all-round entertainers, his story remains proof to the power of hard work, lifelong learning and an unwavering commitment to one’s craft.
